metadata.summary: Dispatcher index mapping task keywords to canonical sibling methodology folders. Every entry is routing pointer, not spec. Load siblings for real content before implementing.

content.title: Software Developer Methodology Index
content.summary: A dispatcher index for the software-developer skill. Not a standalone methodology — maps task keywords to canonical sibling methodology folders under free/dev/software-developer/. Each sibling contains full, authoritative content; this index provides a starting routing point only.

why.p: The legacy umbrella README listed 68 patterns as 5-line stubs, causing agents to produce shallow output by quoting stubs instead of loading the real sibling content. This index encodes the routing table and explicitly forbids agents from implementing directly from it.

list.item: The user gives a coarse task ("implement a Django service", "scaffold a Go API", "write tests for X") and you have not yet selected a sibling methodology folder.
You need the canonical execution order for a multi-language task: Python to JS/TS to backend to DevOps to docs.
You need to verify which methodology slugs actually exist before dispatching parallel subagents.
Implementing from this file directly — every entry is a routing pointer, not a spec.
Treating it as authoritative best-practices guidance — pull current guidance from each sibling.
Citing it as the source of any rule — cite the sibling folder instead.

section.name: trigger
section.p: Apply this index at the very start of any software-developer task, immediately after parsing the user's intent and before you write a single line of code or open a sibling methodology. One pass through the routing table per task; never quote stubs.

section.name: dispatcher-and-routing
section.p: This methodologies/ directory is not a single methodology — it is the legacy umbrella index for the software-developer skill, listing 68 patterns across Python, JS/TS, backend languages, DevOps, and documentation. Most of those patterns now live as their own siblings under free/dev/software-developer/slug/ and are individually enriched. This page is the agent's lookup table: pick the right sibling, do not implement directly from this index.

rule.statement: Always hop to the sibling folder. This index is a map, not a manual. Load the sibling AGENTS.md before implementing anything.
rule.rationale: The stub code in this index is 5-line skeletons. Agents that quote them produce shallow output. The full authoritative content lives one folder away.

rule.statement: Validate agent names against the actual agents/ directory before delegating. Do not use names from historical rows in this index.
rule.rationale: Historical agent names (faion-code-agent, faion-test-agent, faion-devops-agent) do not exist. Only faion-sdd-executor-agent, nero-sdd-executor-agent, and password-scrubber-agent are present.

list.item: Python / Django patterns → django-coding-standards, django-testing
Python modern features (3.12/3.13), async, type hints → best-practices-2026
Go concurrency, goroutines, worker pools → go-concurrency
Test-first development (any language) → tdd-workflow
Storybook, component documentation, visual testing → storybook-setup
CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md creation or audit → documentation
2026 best practices across TS/React/Python/AI → best-practices-2026
DevOps, Docker, K8s, Terraform, CI/CD → pro/infra/ siblings (out of scope here)

section.name: checklist
section.p: Run this checklist literally for every task that hits the software-developer skill. Each step has a verification point — do not skip ahead.

list.item: 1. PARSE — read the user's task; extract language, framework, and primary verb (scaffold, fix, refactor, test, document).
2. ENUMERATE — list real siblings: `ls skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/`. Use this output as ground truth, not the historical "68 methodologies" headline.
3. MATCH — apply the routing-table mapping above; output top 3 candidate sibling slugs with H/M/L confidence and a one-line rationale each.

4. STUB-CHECK — for each candidate, run `wc -l skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/<slug>/AGENTS.md`. If any candidate's AGENTS.md is <30 lines, mark it stub-only and pick a non-stub alternative.
5. LOAD — Read the chosen sibling's AGENTS.md (and methodology.xml if present). Do NOT implement before this Read returns.

6. AGENT-VERIFY — if you plan to delegate, confirm the agent exists: `ls agents/ skills/*/agents/ 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^(faion-sdd-executor-agent|nero-sdd-executor-agent|password-scrubber-agent)\.md$'`. If your target name is missing, fall back to faion-sdd-executor-agent.
7. SDD-FRAME — open or update the SDD lifecycle file (`.aidocs/backlog/ → todo/ → in-progress/ → done/`); never start coding without a feature dir.
8. EXECUTE — implement under the sibling's checklist, not this index's stubs.
9. VERIFY — run sibling-specific tests + the universal post-flight: `ruff check .` (Python) or `npm run typecheck && npm run lint` (TS). Update CHANGELOG.md under `## [Unreleased]`.
10. CITE — in the PR/commit body, cite the sibling slug path, never this index.

section.name: canonical-ordering-for-cross-cutting-tasks
section.p: When a task spans multiple domains, apply siblings in this order: Python ecosystem to JS/TS to other backends to DevOps to docs. This is a sequencing guide, not a priority ranking.

section.name: commands
section.p: Concrete shell commands the agent can copy-paste at each routing step. All paths are relative to the repo root.

list.item: List real siblings (ground truth): `ls -1 skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/ | sort`
Search the index for a keyword: `rg -n -S "django|python|async" skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/methodologies/methodology.xml`
Locate a sibling by slug: `fd -t d "django-coding-standards" skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/`

Detect stub-only siblings (AGENTS.md <30 lines): `for d in skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/*/; do n=$(wc -l < "$d/AGENTS.md" 2>/dev/null || echo 0); [ "$n" -lt 30 ] && echo "STUB: $d ($n lines)"; done`
Verify an agent exists before delegating: `ls agents/ skills/*/agents/ 2>/dev/null | grep -F "${AGENT}.md" || echo "MISSING — fall back to faion-sdd-executor-agent"`
Find recently added stubs: `git log --diff-filter=A --since="90 days ago" -- 'skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/*/AGENTS.md'`
Validate this file after editing: `python3 scripts/validate-methodology-xml.py skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/methodologies/methodology.xml`

section.name: dispatch-prompt-template
section.p: Copy-paste prompt for the routing pass. Fill `<USER_TASK>` and feed to a router agent (or run inline). Output is JSON; reject any other shape.

list.item: SYSTEM: You are a methodology router for the faion software-developer skill. You never implement code. You only route.

USER: Task = `<USER_TASK>`. Steps: (1) read `skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/methodologies/methodology.xml`; (2) list candidate sibling slugs from `ls skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/`; (3) map task keywords to siblings via the routing-table section; (4) return JSON `{"candidates":[{"slug":"...","confidence":"H|M|L","rationale":"...","stub_only":true|false}], "order":["slug1","slug2"], "fallback_agent":"faion-sdd-executor-agent"}`.

RULES: never quote stubs from the index; never invent slugs not present in `ls`; mark `stub_only=true` if `wc -l <slug>/AGENTS.md` returns <30; if zero non-stub candidates exist, set `candidates=[]` and `escalate=true`.

section.name: verification
section.p: How the agent confirms the routing pass succeeded before any code is written.

list.item: Routing JSON parses with `jq` and contains at least one candidate with `stub_only=false`, OR `escalate=true` is set.
Each `slug` returned matches an entry in `ls skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/` (no hallucinated slugs).
Sibling AGENTS.md was opened with the Read tool in the same session — verify by checking the conversation tool log.
If delegation is planned, the chosen agent name appears in `ls agents/ skills/*/agents/`; otherwise the plan falls back to `faion-sdd-executor-agent`.
`python3 scripts/validate-methodology-xml.py skills/faion/knowledge/free/dev/software-developer/methodologies/methodology.xml` exits 0 after any edit to this index.
The PR/commit body cites a sibling slug path (e.g., `free/dev/software-developer/django-coding-standards/`) and never `methodologies/methodology.xml` as the source of any rule.

section.name: where-it-fails-limitations
section.p: Stale fragments: Many entries are 5-line skeletons; the real depth is in sibling folders. An agent that only reads this index produces shallow output.

Broken cross-refs: The "Agents" table references names like faion-code-agent, faion-test-agent, faion-devops-agent, faion-frontend-brainstormer-agent, faion-storybook-agent, faion-frontend-component-agent. Most of those agents do NOT exist in this repo's agents/ directory — only faion-sdd-executor-agent, nero-sdd-executor-agent, and password-scrubber-agent are present. Treat the original list as aspirational.
Mixed scope: It conflates language patterns (Python type hints) with infra (Kubernetes ConfigMap), making it a poor single navigation layer for any one domain.
No SDD integration: The original index predates the .aidocs/ lifecycle; an agent following it verbatim skips spec, design, test, impl gates.
Section count drift: Headline says "68 methodologies" but the sibling folder count is different (and growing); use folder listings as ground truth.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use this index purely as a dispatcher. The recommended Claude flow: (1) parse the user's task; (2) match keywords against the routing table; (3) read the sibling folder's AGENTS.md, checklist.md, and agent-integration.md (where it exists) — those are the real source of truth; (4) execute under the SDD lifecycle (backlog/ to todo/ to in-progress/ to done/) using faion-sdd-executor-agent. Never quote stub snippets from this index in production code; always hop to the sibling.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent drives the actual implementation cycle once the right methodology has been resolved. password-scrubber-agent scrubs any DevOps-section snippets (Terraform state, AWS IAM, secret-management) before sharing externally. A purpose-built methodology-router agent (worth creating): takes a free-text task, returns the sibling folder slug(s) plus a confidence score; refuses to dispatch into stub-only entries. nero-sdd-executor-agent — when the dispatched task lands on a NERO repo, this agent applies the .product/ lifecycle instead of the generic .aidocs/.

Dispatch pass: Given the user task, consult the routing table and return (1) top 3 candidate sibling slugs (full path under free/dev/software-developer/), (2) for each, confidence (H/M/L) and one-line rationale, (3) recommended order if more than one applies, (4) flag any candidate whose sibling folder has only stub content (≤30 lines of README) — escalate to a human or pick another. Do NOT implement; only route.

Read-the-real-doc pass: For the resolved sibling slug, load the full content from the sibling folder. Then proceed under the SDD lifecycle. Reject any plan that quotes this index's stubs instead of the sibling content.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Tools for searching and auditing sibling methodologies.

list.item: rg (ripgrep) — search slugs and section headers across the index and sibling folders. apt install ripgrep; https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep
fd — locate sibling folders by slug. apt install fd-find; https://github.com/sharkdp/fd
glow / mdcat — render markdown in terminal for fast review. https://github.com/charmbracelet/glow
tree -L 2 — visualize sibling structure under free/dev/software-developer/. bundled
markdownlint-cli2 — lint stub-quality of sibling READMEs. https://github.com/DavidAnson/markdownlint-cli2
git log --diff-filter=A -- slug/README.md — determine when a methodology was added; flag old stubs. bundled with git

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Platforms and services for organizing and discovering methodologies.

list.item: GitHub Actions — periodic job to detect stub methodologies (less than 30 lines) and open issues.
meilisearch / typesense — OSS search. Index all sibling READMEs; let the dispatcher agent query semantically instead of regex.
Linear / GitHub Issues — SaaS issue tracker. Track each stub-quality methodology as a backlog item to enrich.
ChromaDB / Qdrant — OSS vector DB. Embed the index and siblings for RAG-style routing.
Always hop to the sibling. This index is a map, not a manual. The depth is one folder away.

Verify the agent table before delegating. The historical "Agents" rows reference names that may not exist; check agents/ and skills/*/agents/ first. If an agent is missing, fall back to faion-sdd-executor-agent.
Use it for ordering, not content. The Python to JS to backend to DevOps to docs sequence is a sensible default for full-stack scaffolding tasks.
Pair with best-practices-2026/. When this index says "Python Type Hints," cross-read the 2026 file for current syntax (| unions, TypeIs, ReadOnly).
Keep it as the routing layer. Resist the urge to expand it back into a textbook — fragmenting into siblings was the right call.
Audit quarterly. Run the stub-detector; sibling READMEs less than 80 lines are likely candidates for the next research batch.
Mark deprecations explicitly. When a methodology is superseded (e.g., generic "React Component Pattern" to React 19 RSC patterns), edit this index to point to the replacement.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Stub seduction: Agents read the 5-line code samples in this index and treat them as complete implementations, producing shallow output without loading sibling content. This index was intentionally kept as stubs. Implementing from stubs is guaranteed to miss edge cases, error handling, and project conventions documented in siblings. Force a sibling read before generating any code. Reject prompts that quote this index's stubs instead of the sibling content.

Hallucinated agent names: Agent calls Task(subagent_type="faion-code-agent", ...) or similar because the index lists that name. These agent names are aspirational. The call fails at runtime. Only faion-sdd-executor-agent, nero-sdd-executor-agent, and password-scrubber-agent exist.

Outdated framework references: Agent quotes the generic "Next.js App Router" or "React Component Pattern" snippets from the legacy README and ships 2-year-old patterns. Current guidance is in best-practices-2026. The legacy index predates React 19, TypeScript 5, and Python 3.12.

Section count drift: Agent trusts the headline "68 methodologies" figure and assumes it reflects the current sibling folder count. Use ls to count sibling folders; the headline figure drifts as new methodologies are added or removed.

DevOps scope bleed: Agent routes Kubernetes / Terraform work through this software-developer index instead of pro/infra/ siblings. DevOps patterns here predate the pro/infra/* skill expansion and are stub-quality. Use the correct tier.
No SDD lifecycle awareness: Agents pulled in by this index skip spec/design/test docs and dive into code. Mandate the lifecycle in the dispatcher prompt.
Frontend-only / backend-only blind spot: Tasks that span both (e.g., Storybook + Next.js + Django API) require multiple sibling reads; the agent must pick a set, not a single slug.
Trusting the count: "68 methodologies" is the historical figure; current tally differs. Use ls not the README's number.
